original_abstract: Science is instrumental to evidence-based decision-making, particularly during global crises. Public trust in scientists facilitates the translation of research into effective policy; however, recent challenges to the epistemic authority of science have fueled concerns about a global decline in trust. We investigated these concerns using a pre-registered survey of 71,922 respondents across 68 countries. Our findings reveal that in most nations, a large majority of the public trusts scientists and supports their increased involvement in society and policymaking. While we observed variations in trust levels both between and within countries—driven by individual and country-level factors such as political orientation and religiosity—there is no evidence of a widespread crisis of trust. Nevertheless, the lack of confidence among even a small minority remains significant, as it can hinder the integration of scientific evidence into the political process. These results offer a nuanced perspective for scientists and policymakers aiming to strengthen the relationship between science and the public.
humanized_abstract: Science underpins many decisions that policymakers have to make, especially in times of global crisis. For those decisions to be made on the basis of evidence, it is important that the public trusts scientists enough to translate their findings into policy. This trust has come under threat in recent years, however, fueling speculation about a global science trust crisis. Our pre-registered survey of 71,922 people in 68 countries finds that, for the vast majority of countries, a large majority of the public trust scientists, believe that scientists should become more involved in society and policy-making, and endorse a set of institutional arrangements intended to strengthen the relations between science and society. While trust varies between countries and between individuals within countries — reflecting factors such as party affiliation and religiosity — our results show no sign of a global science trust crisis. Even if a minority is untrustful, this is enough to make a difference to the politics of science — as policymakers know only too well. Our results provide scientists and policymakers with a set of new tools with which to think about the relations between science and society.

While trust varies between countries and between individuals within countries — reflecting factors such as party affiliation and religiosity — our results show no sign of a global science trust crisis. Even if a minority is untrustful, this is enough to make a difference to the politics of science — as policymakers know only too well. Our results provide scientists and policymakers with a set of new tools with which to think about the relations between science and society.
